nativeRange.habitat: The Rusty Cichlid is endemic to Boadzulu, Chinyankwazi, and Chinyamwezi Islands in the southeastern arm of Lake Malawi, where it inhabits surge-washed boulder fields and rocky reefs in clear, hard, alkaline water at depths of roughly 1 to 15 metres.

decorPreferences: stacked rockwork with caves and crevices for territory and refuge
open sandy floor areas for natural foraging behaviour
minimal or no plants (not a planted-tank species)
smooth substrate (fine sand or fine gravel) to protect ventral surfaces

diet.notes: Omnivore with a herbivorous lean; the diet foundation should be high-quality spirulina flakes, algae wafers, and blanched vegetables (spinach, zucchini). Small live and frozen foods (brine shrimp, daphnia, cyclops) can be offered as supplements. Bloodworms and other high-protein meaty foods should be fed only sparingly — excess protein is associated with Malawi bloat in this species. In the wild the fish grazes on aufwuchs (algae, diatoms, and associated micro-invertebrates) scraped from rocky surfaces.

goodWith: Aulonocara (peacock cichlids) — similar water needs, less competitive
Copadichromis and other open-water Malawi haplochromines
other mild mbuna of similar size (e.g., Cynotilapia afra)
avoidWith: large aggressive mbuna (Melanochromis, Pseudotropheus socolofi) — will outcompete and bully
Petrotilapia and other dominant rock-territory mbuna
small peaceful community fish — mbuna water chemistry incompatible
any fish unable to hold its own in a Malawi setup
Tanganyikan cichlids requiring softer or more neutral water

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by Gerard Delany (2006), 536x391. Sharp lateral view of a live specimen clearly showing the diagnostic rusty-orange head grading into the lavender-purple flank; whole fish in frame among rockwork.
Photo by Michael K. Oliver, 360x173. Most vibrant mature male, full lateral profile with intense rust-red head and bright lavender-purple body showing both diagnostic colours; caveat: low resolution.
Photo by Gerard Delany (2006), 520x333. Head-on portrait of a live specimen showing lavender-blue face, rusty tones and blue-ringed eyes; kept as a variety extra (frontal framing).

summary: The Rusty Cichlid is widely considered the least aggressive of the mbuna — the rock-dwelling cichlids of Lake Malawi — making it a popular entry point for the group. Endemic to a few islands in the lake’s southeastern arm, it reaches roughly 10 cm and shows rusty-orange to lavender-purple colouration. A maternal mouthbrooder, it breeds readily in captivity.
careNotes: The Rusty Cichlid (Iodotropheus sprengerae) is widely considered the least aggressive of the mbuna — the rock-dwelling cichlid group native to Lake Malawi — and is commonly recommended as a starting point for aquarists new to the group. Despite its comparatively mild temperament, it remains a cichlid and will establish territories among rockwork, so adequate caves and visual barriers are essential. A tank with at least a 90 cm footprint (approximately 120 L) suits a single-male group of one male and two to three females; for multiple males or a mixed mbuna community, a minimum 120 cm footprint is generally advised, which also reduces the risk of the fish being outcompeted by more assertive species. The Rusty Cichlid tends to be sensitive to Malawi bloat — a digestive condition associated with overfeeding protein-rich meaty foods — so the diet should be anchored in plant matter and spirulina flakes. Bloodworms in particular should be limited to occasional small portions. Water quality must be kept high: Lake Malawi is exceptionally clear and nutrient-poor, so nitrate accumulation is poorly tolerated, and regular water changes of 25–30% per week alongside efficient filtration are important. Hard, alkaline water (pH 7.5–8.8, general hardness 10–25) is essential; soft or acidic conditions cause rapid health decline. Males develop a characteristic lavender-purple sheen at maturity, while juveniles and females retain the rusty-brown colouration that gives the species its common name. The species is classified as Near Threatened by the IUCN (2018) due to its extremely restricted endemic range.
breedingNotes: The Rusty Cichlid is a polygamous maternal mouthbrooder that tends to breed readily in well-maintained aquaria. Males court females through lateral displays and shimmying behaviour around rocky territory. Females carry fertilised eggs and fry in their mouths for approximately 21 days before releasing free-swimming young. Clutch sizes range from 5 to 60 fry, with larger females generally producing larger broods. The species reaches sexual maturity at roughly 3.8 cm. A holding female should ideally be moved to a separate container to avoid harassment from tankmates; if that is not possible, dense rockwork must provide enough cover for her to hide undisturbed until release. Fry are released at a relatively advanced size and can accept finely crushed flake food or newly hatched brine shrimp nauplii from the start. No paternal care is provided. The species hybridises readily with other mbuna if housed carelessly with closely related species.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Iodotropheus sprengerae
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (max 10 cm wild, ~7.5 cm captivity), temperature 25–29°C, pH 7.5–8.8, hardness 10–25°H, minimum tank dimensions (90×30×30 cm, 80 L for single-male group; 120 cm length for multiple males), diet (omnivore with vegetable foundation), temperament (least aggressive mbuna), compatible genera, native range (Boadzulu, Chinyankwazi, Chinyamwezi Islands, southern Lake Malawi), breeding (maternal mouthbrooder, 3-week hold, 5–60 fry, mature at 1.5").

additional.name: FishBase — Iodotropheus sprengerae
additional.notes: Cross-checked maximum size (10.8 cm SL), temperature range (24–26°C), native range (Lake Malawi, Boadzulu and Chinyankwazi Islands, also Chinyamwezi Island), taxonomy (Cichlidae, Cichliformes, Pseudocrenilabrinae), reproduction (female mouthbrooder), conservation status (Near Threatened). FishBase temperature range 24–26°C is narrower than SeriouslyFish’s 25–29°C; upper bound from SeriouslyFish retained as the more trade-applicable care range.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Iodotropheus sprengerae
additional.notes: Confirmed maximum size (10.8 cm SL), sexual dichromatism (juveniles and females rusty-brown; mature males lavender-purple), endemic range restricted to islands and reefs in southeastern arm of Lake Malawi, omnivore diet (meaty foods linked to digestive problems), mouthbrooding reproduction, and “one of the least aggressive mbuna” characterisation. Named in honour of Kappy Sprenger; described by Oliver & Loiselle 1972.

additional.notes: Image candidate source. Photograph by Gerard Delany, CC BY-SA 2.5. Used to populate imageCandidates.

fish.breedingNotes: Females carry fertilised eggs and fry in their mouths for approximately 21 days, releasing clutches of 5 to 60 free-swimming young. The species reaches sexual maturity at roughly 3.8 cm. No specialised equipment is required beyond adequate rockwork and a ratio of at least three females per male. Moving holding females to a separate container reduces stress and improves fry survival.
